1: Iconium was a major city of the Phrygian-Lycaonian border region in central Anatolia, an important node on the road system connecting the Anatolian plateau cities. It had a substantial Jewish community and a mixed Greek-speaking Gentile population. The pattern — synagogue entry → large Jewish and Gentile response → Jewish opposition → forced departure — is the paradigmatic first-journey sequence Luke repeats at each city, establishing a narrative template for the Pauline mission strategy.
2: Jews who refused to believe (hoi de apeithēsantes Ioudaioi) stirred up the Gentiles and poisoned their minds (ekakōsan tas psychas tōn ethnōn) against the brothers: the construction of organized opposition by the synagogue community against new converts reflects the social dynamics of diaspora Jewish communities. The synagogue held considerable communal authority; its leadership's hostility could translate into civic pressure, economic exclusion, and eventually mob action — precisely what develops in Acts 14:4-5.
3: So they remained for a long time, speaking boldly for the Lord, who bore witness to the word of his grace by granting signs and wonders to be done through their hands: the apostolic decision to stay despite opposition is notable. Signs and wonders (sēmeia kai terata) authenticating the word is the pattern established at Pentecost (Acts 2:43; Acts 5:12) and Philip's Samaritan mission (Acts 8:6-7). God's verification through miracles is what enables the mission to continue in a hostile environment.
4: The city was divided (eschisthē to plēthos tēs poleōs): the gospel's arrival creates social fissure rather than uniform acceptance. The division follows existing social allegiances — some with the Jews, others with the apostles. Luke's use of 'apostles' for both Paul and Barnabas here (and Acts 14:14) reflects a broader usage of the term than Luke normally employs (restricted to the Twelve in most of Acts); it acknowledges Paul and Barnabas as commissioned agents of the Antioch church.
5: An attempt by both Gentiles and Jews with their rulers to mistreat and stone them (hybrisai kai lithobolēsai autous): the coalition of Jewish and Gentile opposition united under civic leadership is significant. The stoning threat uses the Jewish judicial method of execution for blasphemy (Leviticus 24:14; cf. Stephen); its adoption by Gentile civic rulers reflects the kind of cross-community enforcement that threatened both Roman public order charges and Jewish religious charges.
6: They fled to Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia: Lystra and Derbe were in the Roman province of Galatia (Lycaonia region). Lystra was a Roman colony (founded by Augustus) with a relatively small Jewish community. The absence of a synagogue (no mention of one) suggests Paul's initial audience there was primarily Gentile — the healing of the lame man (Acts 14:8-10) serves as the public sign that opens the mission. The Galatian letter likely addresses this region.
7: And there they continued preaching the gospel: the brief summary before the Lystra healing is Luke's signal that the mission continues despite forced relocation. The plural 'there' suggests itinerant activity across the region; the Lystra healing narrative focuses the mission at a specific moment and place. Lystra was Timothy's hometown (Acts 16:1-2), suggesting the visit in chapter 14 produced lasting community roots.
8: A man at Lystra who was unable to use his feet, lame from birth, had never walked: the description precisely matches the lame man of Acts 3:2 — congenital lameness, never having walked. The echo is deliberate: Paul's healing miracle mirrors Peter's, establishing that the apostolic healing power is consistently channeled through all authorized agents, not unique to the original Twelve. Luke's parallel establishes the equivalence of Paul's apostleship with Peter's.
9: He listened to Paul speaking. Paul looked directly at him and saw that he had faith to be healed (pistis tou sōthēnai): the capacity to perceive faith is a feature of the healing narratives in Acts (cf. Acts 3:4 — Peter's direct gaze). The faith-as-prerequisite-for-healing pattern echoes the synoptic healings (Mark 5:34; Acts 10:52). The direct gaze (atenisas autō) is the same word used for Peter at the Beautiful Gate — another deliberate parallel.
10: 'Stand upright on your feet!' (Anastēthi epi tous podas sou orthos) — the command uses the same structure as Peter's 'rise and walk' at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3:6). The lame man 'sprang up and began walking' (hēlato kai periepatei): the springing response echoes Isaiah 35:6 ('then the lame man will leap like a deer'), the eschatological restoration sign. The public nature of the healing in the open square — not a private or controlled setting — maximizes its civic impact.
11: When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices in Lycaonian (Lykaonisti): the use of the regional language rather than Greek is a sociological marker — in their excitement, the crowd reverts to the mother tongue. Lykaonisti is the only reference to this Lycaonian dialect in ancient sources; it confirms Luke's acquaintance with regional linguistic diversity. The crowd's reaction — mistaking the apostles for Zeus and Hermes descended — reflects the Anatolian religious environment.
12: They called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, because he was the leader in speaking (hēgeito tou logou): in Greco-Roman divine mythology, Hermes was the messenger and spokesman of the gods — a natural identification for the speaking apostle. A local legend recorded by Ovid (Metamorphoses 8.626-724) describes Zeus and Hermes visiting Phrygia (adjacent to Lycaonia) incognito; the story explains the crowd's readiness to interpret miraculous visitors as divine. They are determined not to repeat their ancestors' inhospitality to the gods.
13: The priest of Zeus whose temple was before the city brought oxen and garlands to the gates and wanted to offer sacrifice with the crowds: the planned sacrifice is a major civic-religious event. Sacrifice before a city's main gate was the protocol for receiving divine visitors. The oxen and garlands (taurous kai stephanous) represent considerable resources — this is not a spontaneous offering but a prepared ritual. The priest of Zeus held both religious and civic status; his involvement signals official municipal participation.
14: When the apostles Barnabas and Paul heard of it, they tore their clothes (diarēxantes ta himatia autōn) and rushed out into the crowd: tearing garments is the Jewish gesture of horror at blasphemy (Matthew 26:65; Mark 14:63). Paul and Barnabas's reaction establishes their Jewish monotheistic identity in contrast to the crowd's polytheistic interpretation. The tearing of garments would have been a strange and alarming gesture to a Lycaonian crowd — its cultural code was legible only within Jewish tradition.
15: 'Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men, of like nature with you (homoiopatheis hymin anthrōpoi)' — the Areopagus speech's natural theology in miniature. Paul's address is calibrated to a Gentile audience with no OT background: he begins with creation (Acts 14:15b), divine sustaining providence (Acts 14:17), and the call to turn from idols. The phrase 'the living God who made heaven and earth and the sea and all that is in them' is the language of the Shema's expanded form (Exodus 20:11; Psalms 146:6) — Jewish creation monotheism presented without Jewish cultural markers.
16: 'In past generations he allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways' (en tais parōcheEmenais geneais eiasen panta ta ethnē poreuesthai tais hodois autōn): the acknowledgment of a pre-evangelistic era of divine forbearance parallels Paul's Areopagus speech (Acts 17:30: 'the times of ignorance God overlooked'). This is not moral relativism but the acknowledgment of a salvation-historical shift: the Gentiles' pre-gospel wandering was permitted but is now called to an end by the gospel's arrival.
17: 'Yet he did not leave himself without witness (amartyron heauton aphēken) — he did good by giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons': natural providence as preparatory revelation. The catalogue — rain, seasons, food, gladness of heart — reflects the Greco-Roman understanding of divine beneficence (euergesía) as the primary evidence for divine existence. The argument is that creation's regular provision is a form of divine self-testimony (martyria) that should have led the nations to seek the Creator. Paul's Lystra speech and Areopagus speech both develop this natural-theology argument.
18: Even with these words they scarcely restrained the crowds from offering sacrifice to them (molis katepausan tous ochlous tou mē thyein autois): the resistance of the Lycaonian crowd to Paul's monotheistic correction shows the depth of their polytheistic reflex. A crowd that has witnessed a genuine miracle and identified it as divine action will not easily be redirected from sacrifice. The 'scarcely' (molis) is a realistic detail — the religious enthusiasm of eyewitnesses to a miracle is not quickly dissolved by theological argument.
19: Jews came from Antioch and Iconium and persuaded the crowds to stone Paul (lithasantes ton Paulon eilkon exō tēs poleōs): the traveling Jewish opponents who follow Paul across cities are a historically plausible group — organized religious opponents with resources for travel, motivated by the disruption Paul causes to established diaspora Jewish communities. The stoning of Paul and the dragging outside the city is the same treatment given Stephen (Acts 7:58). Paul lists this stoning in 2 Corinthians 11:25 among his sufferings.
20: When the disciples gathered around him, he rose up and entered the city: the prayer-encircling of Paul's apparently dead body by the disciples is the apostolic community's response. Paul's recovery — whether miraculous or natural — is not explained; Luke records that he rose and returned to the city. The courage of returning to the city where he had just been stoned is remarkable. The next day he left for Derbe with Barnabas, completing the forward thrust of the journey before the return arc begins.
21: After they had preached the gospel to Derbe and made many disciples (mathēteusantes hikanous), they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch: the return journey retraces the route through hostile territory. The decision to return rather than travel east to Tarsus (Paul's hometown, which would have been closer) shows the pastoral priority of strengthening the new communities. Mathēteusantes (having made disciples) is the same verb as Matthew 28:19 — the Great Commission vocabulary applied to the first missionary journey's fruit.
22: Strengthening the souls of the disciples, encouraging them to continue in the faith, and saying that through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God (dia pollōn thlipseōn dei hēmas eiselthein eis tēn basileian tou theou): the 'must' (dei) of tribulation-before-kingdom is the pattern of the Servant's path (Isa 53), Jesus's passion prediction (Luke 9:22), and the Pauline theology of cruciform living (Philippians 3:10; Colossians 1:24). Paul returns to communities he left in persecution and teaches that their suffering is structurally continuous with the apostolic and dominical pattern.
23: They appointed elders for them in every church (cheirotOnēsantes de autois kat ekklēsian presbyterous): cheirotoneō (appoint by raising hands) was the Athenian democratic vocabulary for election by show of hands; in Jewish communities, elders (presbyteroi) were the established governance structure of synagogue communities. The appointment of elders in each new church replicates the Jewish community governance model in the new Christian assemblies — providing pastoral oversight after the apostolic founders depart.
24: They passed through Pisidia (Pisidian region in southern Anatolia) and came to Pamphylia on the southern coast. The journey back through these regions without extended ministry stops (unlike the forward journey) reflects the return-arc narrative structure: the cities named are checkpoints on the route rather than new mission fronts. Pamphylia was the coastal province through which they would access the port of Attalia for the sea voyage back to Antioch.
25: When they had spoken the word in Perga (lalesantes en Pergē ton logon): a brief note distinguishing the return visit (with proclamation) from the earlier passing through (Acts 13:13-14, where they passed through Perga without apparent extended ministry). Attalia (modern Antalya, Turkey) was the major port city of Pamphylia — the staging point for the sea journey across the northeastern Mediterranean to Syria. The return sea voyage completes the geographic arc of the first journey.
26: From Attalia they sailed to Antioch, where they had been commended to the grace of God for the work that they had fulfilled (eis ho ēsan paradedomenoi tē chariti tou theou eis to ergon ho eplērōsan): the commission language echoes the sending scene of Acts 13:2-3. The Antioch church had commended Paul and Barnabas to God's grace; they return having 'fulfilled' (plēroō) the work. The return to the commissioning base for report (Acts 14:27) is the protocol for sent envoys in both Jewish and Greco-Roman diplomatic practice.
27: When they arrived and gathered the church together, they declared all that God had done with them (anēggellon hosa epoiēsen ho theos met autōn) and how he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles: the mission report uses the standard apostolic attribution — all that 'God had done' through them, not what they had accomplished. The 'door of faith' (thyran pisteōs) metaphor (cf. 1 Corinthians 16:9; 2 Corinthians 2:12; Colossians 4:3) frames the Gentile mission as an opening that God created, not a strategic initiative of the apostles.
28: They remained there no little time with the disciples: the extended Antioch stay between the first and second journeys is the recovery and consolidation period. Acts 15:1-2 introduces the circumcision controversy that disrupted this period and required the Jerusalem Council. The Syrian Antioch church, with its mixed Jewish-Gentile composition, was both the theological flashpoint for the Gentile-mission questions and the base from which Paul would continue his ministry.
